questions.stem: A logistic population subjected to constant-effort harvesting is modelled by
questions.explanation: Constant-effort (proportional) harvesting removes individuals at a rate proportional to the current population, so the harvest term is $EN$, where $E$ is the harvesting effort. This gives $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - EN$. Removing a fixed amount per unit time instead would give the constant-yield term $-H$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: A logistic population harvested at a constant yield (a fixed number of individuals removed per unit time) is modelled by
questions.explanation: Constant-yield harvesting removes a fixed quantity $H$ per unit time regardless of the population size, so the harvest term is simply the constant $-H$. This gives $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - H$. The proportional alternative, $-EN$, removes more when the stock is large and less when it is small.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: For constant-effort harvesting $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - EN$ with $0 < E < r$, the nonzero equilibrium population is
questions.explanation: Factor the right side as $N\left[r\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - E\right] = 0$. The nonzero root sets the bracket to zero: $r\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) = E$, so $1 - \frac{N}{K} = \frac{E}{r}$ and $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$. This is positive precisely when $E < r$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: Under constant-effort harvesting, what happens to the population when the effort reaches or exceeds the intrinsic growth rate, that is $E \ge r$?
options: The only nonnegative equilibrium is $N^* = 0$, so the population is driven to extinction.
questions.explanation: The nonzero equilibrium $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$ becomes zero when $E = r$ and negative (hence biologically meaningless) when $E > r$. So for $E \ge r$ the only admissible equilibrium is $N^* = 0$, and since the per capita growth $r\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - E$ is then negative for all $N > 0$, the population declines to extinction.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: Under constant-effort harvesting, the sustainable yield (harvest rate at equilibrium) as a function of effort is $Y(E) = E N^*$. Substituting $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$ gives
questions.explanation: At a positive equilibrium the harvest rate equals the effort times the equilibrium stock, $Y(E) = E N^* = E K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$. This is a downward parabola in $E$, vanishing at $E = 0$ (no effort) and at $E = r$ (stock collapses to zero). Its peak between those endpoints is the maximum sustainable yield.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: The sustainable-yield curve $Y(E) = EK\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$ is maximised at the effort
questions.explanation: Differentiate $Y(E) = KE - \frac{KE^2}{r}$ with respect to $E$: $Y'(E) = K - \frac{2KE}{r} = K\left(1 - \frac{2E}{r}\right)$. Setting $Y'(E) = 0$ gives $E = \frac{r}{2}$, and since $Y''(E) = -\frac{2K}{r} < 0$ this is a maximum. Equivalently, the parabola peaks midway between its roots $E = 0$ and $E = r$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: At the maximum sustainable yield under constant-effort harvesting (effort $E = r/2$), the equilibrium stock is held at
questions.explanation: Substitute $E = r/2$ into $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right) = K\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{K}{2}$. Maximum sustainable yield is therefore obtained when the stock is held at half the carrying capacity, exactly where the unharvested logistic growth rate is greatest.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: The maximum sustainable yield (MSY), the peak value of $Y(E) = EK\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$, equals
questions.explanation: Evaluate $Y$ at the optimal effort $E = r/2$: $Y\left(\frac{r}{2}\right) = \frac{r}{2}\,K\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{r}{2}\cdot K \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{rK}{4}$. This is the largest harvest the population can supply indefinitely; it coincides with the peak of the unharvested logistic growth curve.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: A fishery follows the logistic model with $r = 0.6$ per year and $K = 1000$ tonnes under constant-effort harvesting. The maximum sustainable yield is
questions.explanation: The MSY is $\frac{rK}{4} = \frac{0.6 \times 1000}{4} = \frac{600}{4} = 150$ tonnes per year. It is achieved at effort $E = r/2 = 0.3$ per year, holding the stock at $N^* = K/2 = 500$ tonnes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: A logistic stock has $r = 0.8$ per year and $K = 500$. Under constant-effort harvesting with effort $E = 0.4$ per year, the equilibrium population is
questions.explanation: Use $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right) = 500\left(1 - \frac{0.4}{0.8}\right) = 500\left(1 - 0.5\right) = 500 \times 0.5 = 250$. Since $E = 0.4 = r/2$ here, this effort is exactly the MSY effort and the stock sits at $K/2 = 250$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: A logistic stock has $r = 0.6$ per year and $K = 1000$. Under constant-effort harvesting with effort $E = 0.2$ per year, the sustainable yield $Y = E N^*$ is approximately
questions.explanation: First $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right) = 1000\left(1 - \frac{0.2}{0.6}\right) = 1000 \times \frac{2}{3} \approx 666.7$. Then $Y = E N^* = 0.2 \times 666.7 \approx 133.3$ per year. This is below the MSY of $\frac{rK}{4} = 150$, consistent with $E = 0.2$ being less than the optimal $r/2 = 0.3$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: For constant-effort harvesting, the nonzero equilibrium $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$ (with $0 < E < r$) is
options: unstable, since a harvested population always collapses
stable, because the linearised growth rate there is $-(r - E) < 0$
questions.explanation: Write $f(N) = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - EN$, so $f'(N) = r\left(1 - \frac{2N}{K}\right) - E$. At $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$ this evaluates to $-(r - E)$, which is negative whenever $E < r$. A negative slope means perturbations decay, so the harvested equilibrium is stable; meanwhile $f'(0) = r - E > 0$ makes extinction unstable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: Consider constant-yield harvesting $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - H$. The equilibria are the roots of
questions.explanation: Set $rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - H = 0$, that is $rN - \frac{r}{K}N^2 - H = 0$. Multiplying by $-1$ and ordering by powers of $N$ gives $\frac{r}{K}N^2 - rN + H = 0$, a quadratic. Its two roots are the two equilibria of the constant-yield model.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: Solving $\frac{r}{K}N^2 - rN + H = 0$, the two equilibria of the constant-yield model can be written as
questions.explanation: The quadratic formula applied to $\frac{r}{K}N^2 - rN + H = 0$ gives $N = \dfrac{r \pm \sqrt{r^2 - 4(r/K)H}}{2r/K} = \dfrac{K}{2}\left(1 \pm \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4H}{rK}}\right)$. The two real roots exist only while the discriminant $1 - \frac{4H}{rK}$ is nonnegative, that is for $H \le \frac{rK}{4}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: As the constant yield $H$ is increased, the two equilibria of $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - H$ approach each other and collide, after which no positive equilibrium exists. This qualitative change is a
questions.explanation: Two equilibria (one stable, one unstable) merging and then disappearing as a parameter varies is the defining signature of a saddle-node, or fold, bifurcation. A transcritical bifurcation would have the equilibria exchange stability rather than vanish, and a Hopf bifurcation produces oscillations, neither of which happens here.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: The critical yield $H_c$ at which the saddle-node bifurcation occurs in the constant-yield model is
questions.explanation: The two equilibria coincide when the discriminant $1 - \frac{4H}{rK}$ vanishes, that is at $H_c = \frac{rK}{4}$. Equivalently, the largest constant yield the population can balance equals the peak of the logistic growth curve, $\frac{rK}{4}$, reached at $N = K/2$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: For the constant-yield model with $r = 1$ per year and $K = 100$, suppose $H = 16$. The two equilibria are
questions.explanation: Use $N_\pm = \frac{K}{2}\left(1 \pm \sqrt{1 - \frac{4H}{rK}}\right) = 50\left(1 \pm \sqrt{1 - \frac{64}{100}}\right) = 50\left(1 \pm \sqrt{0.36}\right) = 50(1 \pm 0.6)$. This gives $N_- = 50 \times 0.4 = 20$ and $N_+ = 50 \times 1.6 = 80$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: For the constant-yield model with $r = 1$ and $K = 100$, the critical yield at which the population is on the brink of collapse is
questions.explanation: The critical yield is $H_c = \frac{rK}{4} = \frac{1 \times 100}{4} = 25$. At $H = H_c$ the two equilibria coincide at the single value $N = K/2 = 50$, and any $H > 25$ removes more than the population can grow at every $N$, forcing a crash.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: In the constant-yield model with two positive equilibria $N_- < N_+$, their stability is
questions.explanation: With $f(N) = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - H$, the slope is $f'(N) = r\left(1 - \frac{2N}{K}\right)$, which is positive for $N < K/2$ and negative for $N > K/2$. Since $N_- < K/2 < N_+$, we get $f'(N_-) > 0$ (unstable) and $f'(N_+) < 0$ (stable). The lower equilibrium is thus a threshold below which the stock collapses.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: Why is constant-yield harvesting near its critical value $H_c = \frac{rK}{4}$ considered dangerous in fishery management, whereas constant-effort harvesting at MSY is comparatively safer?
options: Constant-yield harvesting has no equilibrium at all.
Near $H_c$ the stable and unstable equilibria nearly coincide, so a small disturbance can push the stock below the threshold and trigger an irreversible collapse.
Constant-yield harvesting makes the population grow without bound.
Constant-effort harvesting can never reach the maximum sustainable yield.
questions.explanation: Just below $H_c$ the stable equilibrium $N_+$ and the unstable threshold $N_-$ sit close together near $K/2$, leaving only a narrow margin; a small downward fluctuation can carry the stock past $N_-$, after which growth can no longer match the fixed harvest and the population falls to zero. Under constant effort the harvest scales down as the stock declines, so a positive stable equilibrium persists for all $E < r$ and the collapse is far more gradual and reversible.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: Combining a strong Allee effect (critical depensation) with harvesting can create which qualitative behaviour absent from the plain harvested logistic model?
options: A single globally stable equilibrium for every harvest level
Bistability: a stable extinction state and a stable positive state separated by an unstable threshold
questions.explanation: Under critical depensation the per capita growth rate is negative below a threshold population, so $N = 0$ is itself a stable state. Together with a stable positive equilibrium and an intervening unstable threshold, this gives bistability: the eventual fate depends on the starting stock. Harvesting raises the threshold and shrinks the basin of the positive state, so even a stock that was viable can be tipped into the extinction basin.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: A key practical contrast between constant-effort and constant-yield harvesting is that
options: constant-effort harvesting leaves a positive stable equilibrium for all $E < r$, while constant-yield harvesting loses every positive equilibrium once $H > \frac{rK}{4}$
both strategies collapse the stock at exactly the same parameter value
constant-yield harvesting can never drive a population extinct
constant-effort harvesting removes a fixed number of individuals each year
questions.explanation: Under constant effort the harvest term $EN$ shrinks with the stock, so a positive stable equilibrium $N^* = K\left(1 - \frac{E}{r}\right)$ exists for every $E < r$ and disappears only gradually as $E \to r$. Under constant yield the fixed harvest $H$ does not relent, so once $H$ exceeds the critical $\frac{rK}{4}$ both equilibria vanish in a saddle-node bifurcation and the stock crashes abruptly.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: A logistic stock follows constant-effort harvesting $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - EN$ with $r = 1$ per year and $K = 100$.
questions.solution: The sustainable yield is maximised at the effort $E = r/2 =$ {{1}} per year. At that effort the stock is held at $N^* = K/2 =$ {{2}}, and the maximum sustainable yield is $\frac{rK}{4} =$ {{3}} per year.
questions.explanation: Optimal effort is $E = r/2 = 1/2 = 0.5$ per year. The equilibrium stock there is $N^* = K/2 = 100/2 = 50$, and the peak yield is $\frac{rK}{4} = \frac{1 \times 100}{4} = 25$ per year. These three values are the standard MSY summary for the constant-effort model.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: A fishery follows the logistic model with $r = 0.5$ per year and $K = 2000$ tonnes under constant-effort harvesting.
questions.solution: The maximum sustainable yield is $\frac{rK}{4} =$ {{1}} tonnes per year, achieved at the optimal effort $E = r/2 =$ {{2}} per year while the stock is held at $N^* =$ {{3}} tonnes.
questions.explanation: MSY $= \frac{rK}{4} = \frac{0.5 \times 2000}{4} = \frac{1000}{4} = 250$ tonnes per year. The optimal effort is $E = r/2 = 0.25$ per year, and the corresponding stock is $N^* = K/2 = 1000$ tonnes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: Consider constant-yield harvesting $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - H$ with $r = 1$ per year and $K = 100$.
questions.solution: The equilibria collide when $H$ reaches the critical value $H_c = \frac{rK}{4} =$ {{1}}, and at that point the two equilibria merge at the single population $N =$ {{2}}. The bifurcation occurring there is a {{3}} bifurcation.
questions.explanation: The critical yield is $H_c = \frac{rK}{4} = \frac{1 \times 100}{4} = 25$. There the discriminant vanishes and the roots coincide at $N = K/2 = 50$. Two equilibria meeting and then disappearing is a saddle-node (fold) bifurcation.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: For the constant-yield model with $r = 1$ per year and $K = 100$, take the yield $H = 21$.
questions.solution: Using $N_\pm = \frac{K}{2}\left(1 \pm \sqrt{1 - \frac{4H}{rK}}\right)$, the lower equilibrium is $N_- =$ {{1}} and the upper equilibrium is $N_+ =$ {{2}}. Of these, the lower equilibrium $N_-$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: With $\frac{4H}{rK} = \frac{84}{100} = 0.84$, the square root is $\sqrt{0.16} = 0.4$, so $N_\pm = 50(1 \pm 0.4)$, giving $N_- = 30$ and $N_+ = 70$. Since $N_- = 30 < K/2 = 50$, the slope $f'(N_-) = r(1 - 2N_-/K) > 0$, so $N_-$ is unstable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: A stock follows constant-yield harvesting with $r = 0.4$ per year and $K = 1000$.
questions.solution: The critical (maximum sustainable) yield is $H_c = \frac{rK}{4} =$ {{1}} per year, and at that yield the surviving population sits at $N =$ {{2}}. If the imposed yield exceeds $H_c$, the population is driven to {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Here $H_c = \frac{rK}{4} = \frac{0.4 \times 1000}{4} = 100$ per year, attained at $N = K/2 = 500$. For $H > H_c$ the harvest exceeds the maximum logistic growth $\frac{rK}{4}$ at every $N$, so $\frac{dN}{dt} < 0$ always and the stock declines to extinction.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)

questions.stem: Compare the two harvesting strategies for a logistic stock with intrinsic rate $r$ and carrying capacity $K$.
questions.solution: Under constant-effort harvesting a positive stable equilibrium persists for every effort below the threshold $E = r$, and extinction sets in once the effort reaches {{1}}. Under constant-yield harvesting both equilibria vanish in a fold bifurcation once the yield exceeds the critical value, whose general formula is $H_c =$ {{2}}. Of the two schemes, the one in which the harvest automatically eases as the stock falls (making collapse more gradual) is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Constant-effort extinction occurs at $E = r$, where $N^* = K(1 - E/r) = 0$. The constant-yield fold occurs at $H_c = \frac{rK}{4}$, the peak of the logistic growth curve. Because the effort term $EN$ shrinks with the stock, constant-effort harvesting eases off automatically and collapses more gradually, whereas the fixed yield $H$ does not relent.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Mathematical Biology course (Brauer and Castillo-Chavez)
